input_row.title: Ensure that 'Notify about alerts with the following severity (or higher)' is enabled
input_row.description: Enables emailing security alerts to the subscription owner or other designated security contact.
input_row.rationale: Enabling security alert emails ensures that security alert emails are sent by Microsoft. This ensures that the right people are aware of any potential security issues and can mitigate the risk. Impact: Enabling security alert emails can cause alert fatigue, increasing the risk of missing important alerts. Select an appropriate severity level to manage notifications. Azure aims to reduce alert fatigue by limiting the daily email volume per severity level. Learn more: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/configure-email- notifications#email-frequency.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. From Azure Home select the Portal Menu. 2. Select Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 3. Under Management, select Environment settings. 4. Click on the appropriate Subscription. 5. Click on Email notifications. 6. Under Notification types, ensure that the box next to Notify about alerts with the following severity (or higher) is checked, and an appropriate severity level is selected. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each Subscription.  Audit from Azure CLI Including a Subscription ID at the $0 in /subscriptions/$0/providers, ensure the below command returns "state": "On", and that "minimalSeverity" is set to an appropriate severity level: az account get-access-token --query "{subscription:subscription,accessToken:accessToken}" --out tsv | xargs -L1 bash -c 'curl -X GET -H "Authorization: Bearer $1" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$0/providers/Microsoft.Security/se curityContacts?api-version=2020-01-01-preview' | jq '.|.[] | select(.name=="default")'|jq '.properties.alertNotifications'  Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 6e2593d9-add6-4083-9c9b-4b7d2188c899 - Name: 'Email notification for high severity alerts should be enabled'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. From Azure Home select the Portal Menu. 2. Select Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 3. Under Management, select Environment settings. 4. Click on the appropriate Subscription. 5. Click on Email notifications. 6. Under Notification types, check box next to Notify about alerts with the following severity (or higher) and select an appropriate severity level from the drop-down menu. 7. Click Save. 8. Repeat steps 1-7 for each Subscription requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI Use the below command to enable Send email notification for high severity alerts: az account get-access-token --query "{subscription:subscription,accessToken:accessToken}" --out tsv | xargs -L1 bash -c 'curl -X PUT -H "Authorization: Bearer $1" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<$0>/providers/Microsoft.Security/ securityContacts/default1?api-version=2017-08-01-preview -d@"input.json"' Where input.json contains the data below, replacing validEmailAddress with a single email address or multiple comma-separated email addresses: [next page] { "id": "/subscriptions/<subscriptionId>/providers/Microsoft.Security/securityContact s/default", "name": "default", "type": "Microsoft.Security/securityContacts", "properties": { "email": "<validEmailAddress>", "alertNotifications": "On", "alertsToAdmins": "On" } } Default Value: By default, subscription owners receive email notifications for high-severity alerts. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security-center/security-center-provide- security-contact-details 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/securitycenter/security-contacts 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/securitycenter/securitycontacts/list 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-incident- response#ir-2-preparation---setup-incident-notification Additional Information: Excluding any entries in the input.json properties block disables the specific setting by default. This recommendation has been updated to reflect recent changes to Microsoft REST APIs for getting and updating security contact information.
input_row.impact: Enabling security alert emails can cause alert fatigue, increasing the risk of missing important alerts. Select an appropriate severity level to manage notifications. Azure aims to reduce alert fatigue by limiting the daily email volume per severity level. Learn more: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/configure-email- notifications#email-frequency.
input_row.reference: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security-center/security-center-provide- security-contact-details 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/securitycenter/security-contacts 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/securitycenter/securitycontacts/list 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-incident- response#ir-2-preparation---setup-incident-notification Additional Information: Excluding any entries in the input.json properties block disables the specific setting by default. This recommendation has been updated to reflect recent changes to Microsoft REST APIs for getting and updating security contact information.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
- The audit field contains detailed instructions for using Azure CLI commands to retrieve the desired configuration settings.
- The information can be directly queried through Azure Resource Manager APIs.
- The command explicitly states how to check if email notifications for alerts are enabled and what settings can be verified.

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_defender_email_notifications_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
The automated program checks the configuration of security alert email notifications in Microsoft Defender for Cloud using Azure APIs or Azure CLI commands. This will involve:

1. **API Endpoint or CLI Command:**
   - **CLI Command:**
     ```bash
     az account get-access-token --query "{subscription:subscription,accessToken:accessToken}" --out tsv | xargs -L1 bash -c 'curl -X GET -H "Authorization: Bearer $1" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subscriptionId>/providers/Microsoft.Security/securityContacts?api-version=2020-01-01-preview' 
     ```
   
2. **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
   - Iterate through all subscriptions in the Azure account to validate the settings for each.

3. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - Check the response for the property: 
     - `"state": "On"` 
     - The value for `minimalSeverity` should be set to an appropriate severity level (e.g., "Low", "Medium", "High").

4. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** 
     - If `alertNotifications` is set to `"On"` and `minimalSeverity` matches the organization's desired severity.
   - **Fail:**
     - If `alertNotifications` is not set to `"On"` or if `minimalSeverity` is not appropriately configured.

5. **Example API Endpoint:**
   - `https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subscriptionId>/providers/Microsoft.Security/securityContacts?api-version=2020-01-01-preview` 

This process allows for a thorough automated check to ensure compliance with the control regarding email notifications for security alerts in Azure.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The classification as Automated is generally correct since the assessment leverages Azure APIs and CLI commands to retrieve configuration settings, which aligns with an automated approach.
- However, the provided CLI command and API endpoint usage are not entirely accurate or optimal for this specific control. The command should focus on querying the security alert notification settings directly through the appropriate Azure Security Center or Microsoft Defender for Cloud APIs, rather than using a generic access token retrieval method followed by a curl command.
- The use of Azure CLI and Azure Resource Manager (ARM) APIs is appropriate, but the specific implementation details need refinement.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 6

- The automation details contain inaccuracies, particularly in the CLI command and API usage. The command provided is overly complex and not directly aligned with best practices for querying security alert configurations.
- The program name `azure_defender_email_notifications_enabled` is somewhat descriptive, but it could be more precise to reflect the security intent more clearly.
- The CLI command should use `az security contact list` to directly retrieve security contact settings, which includes alert notification configurations.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **CLI Command Improvement:** Use a more straightforward and accurate CLI command:
  ```bash
  az security contact list --query "[?alertNotifications=='On' && minimalSeverity=='<desired_severity>']"
  ```
  This command directly queries the security contact settings for alert notifications and severity levels.
  
- **API Endpoint Improvement:** Instead of using a curl command with a manually retrieved token, leverage the Azure CLI's built-in authentication to interact with the API:
  ```bash
  az rest --method get --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subscriptionId>/providers/Microsoft.Security/securityContacts?api-version=2020-01-01-preview"
  ```
  This approach is more secure and aligns with Azure CLI best practices.

- **Documentation and Clarity:** Provide clearer documentation on how to interpret the results of the CLI command or API response, ensuring that auditors understand what constitutes a pass or fail.

- **Alternative Approach:** Consider using Azure Policy to enforce and audit this configuration, which can provide a more scalable and manageable solution for large environments.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the general approach of using automation is correct, the technical inaccuracies in the implementation details reduce confidence in the current assessment's effectiveness.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the CLI command and API usage to align with best practices and ensure technical accuracy.
- Provide clearer documentation and guidance on interpreting results.
- Consider integrating Azure Policy as an alternative or complementary approach to ensure compliance with the control.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The compliance control involves checking the configuration of security alert email notifications in Microsoft Defender for Cloud, which can be efficiently verified using Azure APIs or CLI commands. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, although the second opinion highlights inaccuracies in the specific command details. Azure Resource Manager APIs and Azure CLI provide the necessary capabilities to automate this check by querying the relevant settings directly.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_defender_for_cloud_email_notifications_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Azure Resource/Service:** Microsoft Defender for Cloud
- **API/CLI Method:** Use Azure CLI command `az security alert list` or equivalent ARM API to retrieve alert notification settings.
- **Properties to Check:** Ensure that the `emailNotification` property is enabled and set to the appropriate severity level.
- **Pass Criteria:** The `emailNotification` property is enabled with a severity level set.
- **Fail Criteria:** The `emailNotification` property is disabled or not set to the appropriate severity level.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The disagreement was resolved by acknowledging the second AI's critique of the initial automation details. The decision to automate remains valid, but with refined implementation details focusing on direct querying of the security alert notification settings.
